text: At constant temperature, doubling the volume of an ideal gas (Boyle's Law) will:
explanation: Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ at constant T and n. Doubling V → halving P. Molecules occupy more space, collide with walls less frequently.

text: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states:
options: Total pressure equals the product of partial pressures
Total pressure equals the sum of partial pressures of each component
Gas pressure is inversely proportional to molecular mass
explanation: P(total) = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... The partial pressure Pᵢ = χᵢ × P(total) (mole fraction × total P). Used in respiratory physiology for alveolar gas calculations.

text: A ball thrown horizontally from a cliff:
options: Has increasing horizontal velocity and constant vertical velocity
Has constant horizontal velocity and increasing vertical velocity (due to g)
Has constant total speed throughout flight
Is unaffected by gravity until it hits the ground
explanation: Projectile motion: horizontal component — constant velocity (no horizontal force in vacuum); vertical — v_y increases as g = 9.8 m/s² acts downward. Time in air: t = √(2h/g). Horizontal range = v₀·t.

text: Newton's Second Law: ΣF = ma means:
options: For every action there is equal and opposite reaction
Object at rest stays at rest without net force
Momentum is conserved in all collisions
explanation: Newton's 2nd Law: ΣF = ma. The vector sum of forces = mass × acceleration. It defines force, mass, and motion relationships used in friction, tension, centripetal, and gravity problems on the MCAT.

text: A 60 kg person in an elevator accelerating upward at 2 m/s² (g = 10 m/s²): what does the scale read?
explanation: N − mg = ma → N = m(g + a) = 60(10 + 2) = 720 N. Accelerating upward → apparent weight > true weight. Decelerating downward → apparent weight < true weight. Free fall → apparent weight = 0.

text: Work done by constant force F at angle θ over displacement d:
explanation: W = F·d·cosθ. Force parallel to displacement: W = Fd (max). Force perpendicular: W = 0 (centripetal force does no work). Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔKE.

text: Conservation of mechanical energy (no friction) states:
options: Work by friction = change in KE
explanation: KE + PE = constant in absence of non-conservative forces. Energy converts between forms: PE → KE when falling. With friction: Wfriction = ΔKE + ΔPE (energy lost as heat). MCAT: ½mv² = mgh.

text: Bernoulli's equation (P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant) expresses:
options: Inverse relationship between speed and pressure in a fluid
Conservation of energy per unit volume in ideal fluid flow
How Reynolds number changes with diameter
explanation: Bernoulli is conservation of energy per unit volume for ideal (incompressible, inviscid, laminar) fluid. Where fluid moves faster, pressure decreases. Explains lift (airfoil), venturi effect, atomizer.

text: The continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ for fluid flow expresses:
options: Conservation of mass for incompressible fluids
Viscous drag force on a sphere
explanation: Flow rate Q = A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = constant. Where cross-section decreases, velocity increases. Combined with Bernoulli: faster fluid has lower pressure.

text: Buoyant force on a submerged object equals:
options: Density of object × g × its volume
Pressure at the bottom of the object only
explanation: Archimedes: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g = weight of fluid displaced. Object floats when F_b = object weight; sinks when F_b < weight. A ship floats because it displaces water equal to its total weight.

text: For a simple pendulum (small angle), the period depends on:
explanation: T = 2π√(L/g). Period is independent of mass and amplitude (for small angles). Longer pendulum → longer period. Stronger gravity → shorter period. On the Moon (g/6), T is √6 times longer.

text: Sound waves are longitudinal because:
options: Particle displacement is perpendicular to wave propagation
Particle displacement is parallel to wave propagation
explanation: In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate parallel to wave propagation (compressions and rarefactions). Sound is a longitudinal pressure wave. Transverse waves (light, water waves) have particle motion perpendicular to propagation.

text: When a sound source moves toward a stationary observer (Doppler effect):
options: Frequency is zero above the speed of sound
explanation: Source approaching: wave crests bunch → shorter wavelength → higher frequency observed. Source receding → longer wavelength → lower frequency. f_obs = f_s × (v ± v_obs)/(v ∓ v_s). Red shift: receding stars.

text: The intensity of light from a point source and the inverse square law:
options: I ∝ 1/d², so doubling distance reduces intensity by factor of 4
explanation: I = P/(4πd²). Double the distance → I decreases to 1/4. This applies to sound, gravity, electrostatic force. Intensity level in dB: β = 10 log(I/I₀).

text: Snell's Law (n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂): when light moves from air (n=1) into glass (n=1.5):
options: Light speeds up and bends away from normal
Light slows down and bends toward normal
Light speeds up and bends toward normal
explanation: Going from lower to higher n: speed decreases (v = c/n), angle decreases (bends toward normal). Snell's law: n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂. When n₂ < n₁, at critical angle θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁): total internal reflection.

text: A converging (convex) lens forms a real image when:
options: Object is between focal point and lens
Object is at the focal point
Object is farther from the lens than one focal length
explanation: Thin lens equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i. Converging lens (f > 0): d_o > f → real, inverted image (d_i > 0). d_o < f → virtual, upright, magnified image. Real images can be projected on a screen.

text: Coulomb's Law: the electrostatic force between two point charges:
explanation: F = kq₁q₂/r² = q₁q₂/(4πε₀r²), k ≈ 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel; opposite attract. Same form as gravity but far stronger and can be repulsive.

text: In a series circuit with resistors R₁ and R₂:
explanation: Series: R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + ... (same current, voltages add). Parallel: 1/R_eq = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ ... (same voltage, currents add; R_eq < smallest R).

text: Ohm's Law: V = IR means:
explanation: V = IR (volts = amperes × ohms). Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. For ohmic (linear) materials, R is constant. Non-ohmic devices (diodes, transistors) have non-linear I-V relationships.

text: Nuclear radiation penetrating power in order from least to greatest:
explanation: Alpha particles (He²⁺ nucleus) stopped by paper/skin. Beta particles (electrons) stopped by few mm aluminum. Gamma rays (photons) require cm of lead or meters of concrete. Penetrating power: α < β < γ.

text: A 100g sample of ¹⁴C (t₁/₂ = 5730 years) after 11,460 years:
explanation: 11,460/5730 = 2 half-lives. After 1 t₁/₂: 50g. After 2 t₁/₂: 25g. Remaining = initial × (½)ⁿ where n = number of half-lives. Used in radiocarbon dating.

text: Nuclear fission releases energy because:
options: Products have greater total mass than reactants
Products have less total mass than reactants; mass difference converts to energy (E = mc²)
Energy input is required for fission
Fission only releases energy with a moderator
explanation: Mass defect Δm → E = Δmc². For ²³⁵U fission: enormous energy release. Binding energy per nucleon peaks at Fe-56. Both fission of heavy nuclei and fusion of light nuclei release energy by moving toward this peak.

text: Infrared (IR) spectroscopy identifies functional groups by:
options: Bond stretching/bending vibrations absorbing specific IR wavelengths
Nuclear spin transitions in a magnetic field
explanation: Key IR absorptions: O-H broad ~2500-3300 cm⁻¹ (COOH) or 3200-3550 (alcohol); N-H ~3300; C=O ~1700-1750; C≡N ~2200. Used to identify functional groups. NMR identifies carbon skeleton; mass spec gives molecular weight.

text: In ¹H NMR, a peak shifted downfield (higher δ) indicates:
options: Protons adjacent to electronegative groups or aromatic rings (deshielded)
Equivalent protons at the same position
explanation: Deshielded protons resonate at higher δ (downfield). Typical shifts: alkyl ~0-2 ppm, alkene ~5-6 ppm, aromatic ~7-8 ppm, aldehyde ~9-10 ppm, carboxylic acid ~10-12 ppm. Electronegative groups and aromatic rings reduce electron density around nearby H.

text: Markovnikov's rule predicts that in HX addition to an unsymmetrical alkene:
options: H adds to the more substituted carbon
H adds to carbon with more H atoms; X goes to more substituted carbon (more stable carbocation)
X adds to less substituted carbon
explanation: The proton adds to the carbon bearing more H atoms, forming the more stable (more substituted) carbocation intermediate. X⁻ then adds to that carbocation. Exception: HBr with peroxides (radical mechanism, anti-Markovnikov).
